explanation_md: `std::cin >> x >> y` reads `3` into `x` and `7` into `y`. Then `std::cout << x << y` prints them concatenated without any space — `37`. A common mistake is expecting a space between the two numbers, but `<<` does not add any separators automatically.

prompt_md: Which statement correctly reads an integer from standard input into the variable `n`?
explanation_md: `std::cin` is the input stream and `>>` is the extraction operator that reads *from* the stream *into* a variable. Option (a) incorrectly uses `>>` with `cout` (the output stream), and option (b) incorrectly uses `<<` with `cin` (the input stream). Option (d) sends `n` to output rather than reading into it.

Each `<<` insertion appends its value directly to the output stream with no automatic spacing or newlines. The three insertions print `A`, then `B`, then `C`, all on one line. The final `'\n'` moves the cursor to the next line but does not appear in the output text itself.

options.a: The string literal uses `\n` instead of `std::endl`.
options.d: `main` must return a value other than `0`.

explanation_md: `std::cout` is declared inside `<iostream>`. Without `#include <iostream>` at the top of the file, the compiler cannot find `std::cout` and reports something like `'cout' was not declared in this scope`. Using `'\n'` instead of `std::endl` is fine; both produce a newline.

prompt_md: What is the key difference between `std::endl` and `'\n'` when used with `std::cout`?

options.a: `std::endl` adds two blank lines; `'\n'` adds one.
options.b: `std::endl` flushes the output buffer; `'\n'` does not.
options.d: They are completely identical in every way.

explanation_md: Both `std::endl` and `'\n'` move the cursor to the next line, but `std::endl` also flushes the internal output buffer, which is slower. They produce the same visible result in most programs, but `'\n'` is preferred for performance when printing many lines.
